retrieved_docs.title: Kentucky KASPER and Controlled Substance Prescribing -- Pathophysiology -- Pain Modulation and Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Pain fibers enter the spinal cord at the posterior root ganglia and synapse in the posterior horn. Fibers then cross to the contralateral side and travel up the lateral columns reaching the thalamus and, finally, the cerebral cortex. Repetitive stimulation from prolonged pain can sensitize neurons in the posterior horn of the spinal cord so that a minimal peripheral stimulus results in pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Dejerine-Roussy Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Central Disinhibition
retrieved_docs.content: The ventral posterolateral nucleus of the thalamus has an intrinsic network of GABAergic neurons, which causes the intrinsic inhibition of ventral posterolateral nucleus. Stroke affecting the lateral thalamus causes central disinhibition by the deafferentation of the thalamic nucleus, which causes the activation of cortical areas resulting in pain. The slow return of neuronal function after stroke or trauma explains the timing of the pain. The disinhibition of temperature-sensing fibers (primarily those that sense cold) might be the cause of cold allodynia.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gamma Aminobutyric Acid -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: When an action potential reaches the synaptic terminal of an inhibitory (GABAnergic) interneuron, the action potential induces a change in membrane potential such that voltage-dependent calcium channels (VDCCs) open. Open VDCCs cause an influx of calcium ions into the axon terminal. The increased calcium concentration in the axon terminal after VDCC opening stimulates the movement of GABA-containing synaptic vesicles to approach the distal axon terminal and exocytose, releasing GABA into the synaptic cleft.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebellum -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The Purkinje layer consists of Purkinje cells, which are large Golgi type I neurons. Their dendrites reach the molecular layer and have multiple branches. The axons are long, pass through the granular layer, enter the white matter, acquire a myelin sheath, and terminate in the intracerebellar nuclei. Their collateral branches make synaptic contacts with the basket and stellate cells of the granular layer. Climbing and mossy fibers provide the primary input to the cerebellar cortex. Mossy fibers use glutamate, while the climbing fibers use aspartate as their main excitatory neurotransmitter to provide excitatory signals to the Purkinje cells. The climbing fibers are named so because they travel in the cortex like vine branches on a tree. They represent the terminal ending of the olivocerebellar tracts. The mossy fibers are the terminal branches of all other cerebellar afferent tracts. Each mossy fiber may stimulate thousands of Purkinje cells via multiple branching. [4] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Kentucky KASPER and Controlled Substance Prescribing -- Pathophysiology -- Pain Modulation and Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral and nerves at other levels of the central nervous system are sensitized, resulting in long-term synaptic changes in cortical receptive fields that can result in exaggerated pain perception. This process of chronic afferent input causing increased sensitivity and lower pain thresholds, with the remodeling of the central nociceptive pathways and receptors termed central sensitization.  The consequence may be: Allodynia (exaggerated response to a nonpainful stimulus) Hyperalgesia (excessive pain response to a normal pain stimulus)

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gamma Aminobutyric Acid -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: While in the synaptic cleft, GABA binds to the alpha and beta subunits on GABA receptors, usually found on the postsynaptic neuron (GABA-A receptors) or other presynaptic axon terminals (GABA-B receptors). [11] GABA-A receptors increase chloride influx into the postsynaptic neuron while GABA-B receptors decrease calcium influx and increase potassium efflux on both presynaptic and postsynaptic neurons. Activation of GABA-A receptors causes hyperpolarization of the postsynaptic neuron, generating an IPSP, while GABA-B activation causes presynaptic neurons to become less likely to release neurotransmitter, especially glutamate. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Nucleus Suprachiasmatic -- Nerves -- 1. Afferent Projections and Neurotransmitters
retrieved_docs.content: The RHT receives direct inputs from photosensitive ganglion cells in the retina. By secreting glutamate into the core VIP regions of the suprachiasmatic nucleus, the RHT mediates the photic regulation of circadian rhythmicity. [9] Another neurotransmitter, pituitary adenylate cyclase-activating polypeptide (PACAP), is also found in retinal ganglion cells and helps relay information about light while potentiating glutamate’s action on the SCN. The GHT provides a secondary, indirect photic input that is mediated by both photic and non-photic stimuli such as a motor or behavioral activity. [9] Within the suprachiasmatic nucleus, the GHT and the RHT partial overlap in their innervation. Neurotransmitters involved in the GHT include neuropeptide Y (NPY), GABA, and enkephalin (ENK). NPY is the GHT's primary neurotransmitter, acting directly on pacemaker neurons of the SCN. NPY also acts presynaptically to inhibit GABA-mediated transmission to the SCN. Recent studies have shown that GABA has dual effects on the SCN neurons that are excitatory by day and inhibitory by night. [10] GABA’s nighttime excitatory effect remains poorly understood but is thought to stem from the depolarization of SCN neurons.

retrieved_docs.title: Postictal Seizure State -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A seizure is terminated by a variety of potential mechanisms, including depletion of energy substrate, desensitization of receptors to the excitatory neurotransmitter, depolarization block, desynchronization of the neuronal networks, the effect of inhibitory neuromodulators such as endogenous opiate receptor agonists, and various other possible mechanisms. These mechanisms can be classified as [3] : Mechanisms acting at the cellular level: Energy failure, potassium current, ion gradients Mechanisms acting on local networks: Glutamate depletion, glial buffering of glutamate, the role of gap junctions on synchrony, increased GABA-ergic inhibition, effects of neuromodulators (endocannabinoids, adenosine, and neuropeptide Y) Mechanisms acting remotely to limit excitation and seizure spread: Effect of other cortical regions and subcortical nuclei on modifying ictal activity

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gamma Aminobutyric Acid -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: GABA usually causes inhibition of action potentials by hyperpolarizing postsynaptic neurons and reducing the release of neurotransmitters from presynaptic neurons. The resulting inhibition decreases the activity of the affected cell, which manifests in effects ranging from decreased motor stimulation in motor neurons to decreased cortical activity in regions including the amygdala. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Introduction -- Anatomy of the Synapse
retrieved_docs.content: Presynaptic terminals are the axonal or dendritic tips resembling small round knobs that release neurotransmitters at the synaptic cleft. Transmitter vesicles and mitochondria lie in the presynaptic terminals. Transmitter vesicles contain the neurotransmitters diffusing through the synaptic cleft to reach the next (postsynaptic) neuron. The mitochondria provide the energy that enables neurotransmitter synthesis, release, and reuptake at the presynaptic terminal.

retrieved_docs.title: NP Safe Prescribing of Controlled Substances While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Pathophysiology -- Pain Modulation and Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Pain fibers enter the spinal cord at the posterior root ganglia and synapse in the posterior horn. Fibers then cross to the contralateral side and travel up the lateral columns, reaching the thalamus and, finally, the cerebral cortex. Repetitive stimulation from prolonged pain can sensitize neurons in the posterior horn of the spinal cord so that a minimal peripheral stimulus results in pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Tetanus(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: At the site of inoculation, tetanus spores enter the body and germinate in the wound. [12] Germination needs particular anaerobic conditions, such as dead and devitalized tissue that has low oxidation-reduction potential. After germination, they release tetanospasmin into the bloodstream. [13] This toxin enters the presynaptic terminals in the neuromuscular endplate of motor neurons and destroys a vesicular synaptic membrane protein resulting in the inactivation of inhibitory neurotransmission that usually suppresses motor neuron and muscle activity. This paralyzes muscle fibers. Subsequently, this toxin, via retrograde axonal transport, travels to neurons in the central nervous system, where it also inhibits neurotransmitter release; this occurs approximately 2 to 14 days after inoculation. Since glycine and GABA are major inhibitory neurotransmitters, cells fail to inhibit the motor reflex response to sensory stimulation, causing a tetanic spasm. This can cause such powerful unopposed muscle activity and contraction that bone fractures and muscle tears can occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Dejerine-Roussy Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Central Sensitization
retrieved_docs.content: Central sensitization is the increased synaptic efficacy of the central afferent neurons leads to spontaneous pain or nociception on suboptimal stimulus. A spontaneous bursting pattern of multifocal asynchronous electrical activity has been recorded in the deafferented thalamic nuclei, with the help of microelectrodes, in patients of central post-stroke pain. The N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonists (ketamine) has helped the central pain in animal models. This provides indirect evidence for the damage of central neurons from N-methyl-D-aspartate-receptor activation in central sensitization.

retrieved_docs.title: Prescription of Controlled Substances: Benefits and Risks -- Pathophysiology -- Pain Modulation and Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Pain fibers enter the spinal canal and the spinal cord at the dorsal root ganglia and then synapse in the dorsal horn. Fibers cross to the other side and up the lateral columns to the thalamus and then to the cerebral cortex.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosin is broadly related to neurotransmitery, functioning as a key neuromodulator that influences the activity of multiple neurotransmitter systems throughout the nervous system. By interacting with various receptor subtypes, adenosin can modulate synaptic transmission, affect neuronal excitability, and play a role in processes such as sleep regulation, pain perception, and neuroprotection. Understanding the extensive relationship between adenosin and neurotransmitery is essential for appreciating its impact on neural communication and for developing therapeutic strategies that target adenosinergic pathways in neurological and psychiatric disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662
retrieved_docs.content: Gabapentin is classified as a member of the hypnotika a sedativa (hypnotics and sedatives) group. The broader category fyziologické účinky léků (physiological effects of drugs) includes both the hypnotika a sedativa class and the stimulanty centrálního nervového systému (central nervous system stimulants) class. This hierarchy shows that gabapentin’s pharmacological action is part of the overall spectrum of drug effects, which comprises sedative agents as well as stimulatory agents affecting the central nervous system, highlighting the diverse ways in which medications can influence physiological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1371
retrieved_docs.content: Ziconotide is classified as a neuroprotective substance, indicating that it belongs to the group of agents that protect neuronal tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 274
retrieved_docs.content: Antiparkinsonika are linked to gabapentin, indicating a therapeutic or mechanistic relationship between these antiparkinsonian agents and the anticonvulsant gabapentin. Although gabapentin is associated with antiparkinsonika, it is not classified as a hypnotika a sedativa; rather, it falls outside the hypnotic and sedative drug category. The broader concept of fyziologické účinky léků (physiological effects of drugs) encompasses several major drug groups, including hypoglykemika, which lower blood glucose levels; hypnotika a sedativa, which produce sleep‑inducing and calming effects; and stimulanty centrálního nervového systému, which increase central nervous system activity. Understanding how specific agents such as gabapentin relate to these categories and to antiparkinsonika helps clinicians appreciate the complex interplay of drug actions, classifications, and therapeutic uses, thereby supporting more informed decisions in patient care.

Together, these relationships illustrate how specific organic psychiatric conditions and syndromes are organized under broader diagnostic categories and pharmacological classifications, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and understanding of underlying pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Antiparkinsonika and gabapentin are noted to have a relationship, indicating that these two drug categories may be clinically connected, such as through co‑administration, potential drug interactions, or complementary therapeutic use in managing neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2723
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulátory are a class of compounds that influence the activity of the neurotransmitter gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the central nervous system, thereby producing inhibitory effects that can be harnessed for therapeutic purposes such as anxiolysis, sedation, and seizure control. Flurazepam is a member of this class; as a benzodiazepine, it enhances GABA‑mediated neurotransmission and is commonly used as a hypnotic agent for the treatment of insomnia. Recognizing that flurazepam functions as a GABA modulator informs clinicians about its sedative properties, potential for dependence, and the need to consider drug‑drug interactions with other central nervous system depressants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1292
retrieved_docs.content: Psychoanaleptics are a subclass of nervous system drugs, representing medications that act on the nervous system to stimulate or enhance neurotransmission.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1468
retrieved_docs.content: The category of fyziologické účinky léků comprises several distinct subgroups of pharmacologic agents. Among these are látky tlumící činnost CNS, which are substances that depress central nervous system activity, hypoglykemika, which are drugs that lower blood glucose levels, and stimulanty centrálního nervového systému, which are agents that enhance central nervous system function. Together, these subcategories illustrate the diverse ways in which drugs can influence physiological processes, ranging from inhibition of neural activity to regulation of metabolic pathways and stimulation of neural excitability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: Flurazepam is classified both as a GABA modulator and as an anxiolytic, indicating that it exerts its therapeutic effects by enhancing the activity of the inhibitory neurotransmitter GABA and is used primarily to alleviate anxiety symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1537
retrieved_docs.content: Pregabalin is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a member of the “other antiepileptics” category, indicating its role as an antiepileptic medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: The category fyziologické účinky léků includes a range of drug actions on the body, and within this category the subgroup hypnotika a sedativa specifically refers to agents that produce sleep‑inducing or calming effects by acting on the central nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1937
retrieved_docs.content: Antiparkinsonika and gabapentin are linked through a therapeutic relationship, indicating that gabapentin may play a role in the management of conditions related to antiparkinsonian treatment. In addition, gabapentin may be employed in the treatment of رُهاب, a term referring to phobic disorders, suggesting its utility beyond neuropathic pain and seizure control. Recognizing these connections highlights gabapentin’s broader potential applications in both movement‑disorder therapy and the management of certain anxiety‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1331
retrieved_docs.content: The PsycINFO subcluster term “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term)” includes acetylcholine as a member, indicating that acetylcholine is classified within this group of signaling molecules. Acetylcholine also has a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, reflecting its role as a neurotransmitter that participates in neural communication and regulation. Together, these relationships underscore acetylcholine’s placement in the larger category of neurotransmitters and neuroregulators, which is important for organizing and understanding its functions in psychological and neuroscientific research.
